fact: February 14 is exactly 45 days after New Year's Day - that's like counting to 45 every single second for 24 hours straight!
In Antarctica, February 14 falls during the warmest part of summer when temperatures can actually get above freezing and penguins go swimming like it's a beach party.

In many northern climates, cats and other animals often get extra frisky around mid-February because daylight is increasing by about 2-3 minutes every single day, and all that extra light makes them feel more energetic and playful.
February 14 sits right in the middle of Aquarius season, which means if the zodiac were a pizza, this date would be the cheesiest center slice of the water-bearer sign.
In many places, February 14 is typically prime time for earthworms to start wiggling closer to the surface as soil begins its slow thaw from winter's freeze.
In northern regions, the sun usually rises about 1 minute and 20 seconds earlier on February 14 than it did just three days before - that's like nature hitting the fast-forward button on morning!
February 14 falls exactly 35 days before the spring equinox, making it the perfect 'countdown to spring' marker when winter starts losing its grip on the Northern Hemisphere.

In places like Canada and northern Europe, February 14 often marks the time when snow starts making that weird crunchy-squeaky sound under your boots because of specific temperature and crystal changes.
Tree sap typically begins its sneaky underground journey upward around February 14 in temperate climates, even though trees still look completely dead and bare on the outside.
February 14 is day 45 of the year, which means there are exactly 320 days left until New Year's Eve - almost like the year is just getting warmed up!
In the Southern Hemisphere, February 14 arrives during late summer when many flowers are typically dropping their petals and going to seed, making it nature's confetti season.
The moon phase on February 14 changes every year, but when it's a full moon on this date, it creates extra-bright nighttime snow scenes in northern regions that look like glittery alien landscapes.
